factual-accuracy.requirement: States independent director is person who is not executive officer/employee and has no relationship interfering with independent judgment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response defines an independent director as someone who is not an executive officer/employee and who has no direct or indirect relationship that would interfere with the exercise of independent judgment (i.e., would impair independent judgment).

factual-accuracy.requirement: States board must make affirmative determination of each director's independence based on all relevant facts
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that NASDAQ requires the board to assess each director’s independence annually while considering all relevant facts and circumstances, and that the board must exercise judgment beyond bright-line tests.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Defines Family Member as spouse, parents, children, siblings, in-laws (mothers/fathers/sons/daughters/brothers/sisters-in-law), and anyone sharing director''s home (excluding domestic employees)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response defines “Immediate family member” to include spouse, parents, children, siblings, and anyone living in the director’s home, but it does not explicitly include in-laws (mother/father/son/daughter/brother/sister-in-law) or explicitly exclude domestic employees. Those required elements are missing from the definition provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director cannot be currently employed by company or have been employed within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under “No Employment” that the individual cannot be an employee now or have been an employee of the company or its subsidiaries during the past three years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director's Family Member cannot be or have been executive officer of company within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a “Family Member Restrictions” bullet stating the director must not have an immediate family member who is, or was in the past three years, an executive officer of the company or its subsidiaries.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States $120,000 direct compensation threshold for any 12-month period within last 3 years disqualifies independence
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that receiving more than $120,000 in compensation in any consecutive 12-month period within the last three years disqualifies a director from being independent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States board/committee fees, pension benefits under tax-qualified plans, and non-discretionary compensation excluded from $120K threshold
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that compensation over $120,000 excludes “director fees” and “certain retirement benefits,” but it does not explicitly specify board/committee fees, pension benefits under tax-qualified plans, and non-discretionary compensation as the excluded categories. The required three specific exclusions are not all clearly named.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States business relationship disqualification threshold is greater of 5% of recipient company's consolidated gross revenues OR $200,000
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes the business relationship threshold as payments exceeding “$200,000 or 5% of the other's annual revenues,” which does not state the required formulation of the greater of 5% of the recipient company’s consolidated gross revenues OR $200,000.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States business relationship threshold applies to payments in current or any of past 3 fiscal years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that material business dealings are disqualifying when payments exceeded thresholds “in each of the last three fiscal years,” which explicitly covers the current or any of the past three fiscal years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot be current partner of company's outside auditor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says the director cannot be a partner or employee of the company's outside auditor, but it does not state that the director’s family member cannot be a current partner of the outside auditor. The required family-member-specific prohibition is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot have been partner/employee of outside auditor who worked on company's audit within past 3 years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states a “No Recent Auditor Connection” rule that the director cannot currently be, or have been in the past three years, a partner or employee of the company’s outside auditor, which matches the required 3-year auditor employment/partner disqualification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States director or Family Member cannot be/have been executive officer of entity where company's executives serve on that entity's compensation committee (current or within past 3 years)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response includes a disqualification stating the director must not serve on another company’s compensation committee where the company’s executive officers serve on that committee, but it does not state the required “director or family member cannot be/have been an executive officer of an entity where the company’s executives serve on that entity’s compensation committee (current or within past 3 years)” condition. The executive-officer and 3-year lookback elements for the other entity are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States for investment companies under Investment Company Act of 1940, interested person (other than solely due to board service) is not independent
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses NASDAQ independence standards and SEC Rule 10A-3 audit committee requirements, but it never mentions the Investment Company Act of 1940 or the concept that an “interested person” (other than solely due to board service) is not independent. The specific investment company/ICA 1940 disqualification is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States majority independent board required for listed companies, subject to Rule 5615 exemptions (controlled companies, investment companies, asset-backed issuers)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that a majority of the board must be independent for Nasdaq-listed companies and notes exemptions for controlled companies. However, it does not mention Rule 5615 or the specific Rule 5615 exempt categories of investment companies and asset-backed issuers.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States audit, compensation, and nominating committees must be composed entirely of independent directors (with limited exemptions)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Audit Committee and Compensation Committee must be composed entirely of independent directors and that nomination oversight must be through a nominating committee composed solely of independent directors (or equivalent independent oversight), and it also notes exemptions for controlled companies and IPO phase-ins.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Defines controlled company as one where more than 50% of voting power held by individual, group, or another company
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions controlled companies as those where a single shareholder or group controls the majority of voting power, but it does not explicitly define this as “more than 50% of voting power held by an individual, group, or another company.” It also omits the “another company” holder type and the specific >50% threshold wording.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States controlled companies exempt from majority independent board and independent compensation/nominating committee requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that controlled companies are exempt from some or all independent director board composition requirements, which includes the majority independent board and independent compensation/nominating committee requirements.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States controlled companies still must maintain audit committee meeting SEC Rule 10A-3 independence requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that controlled companies are exempt from some independence requirements, but it does not state that controlled companies still must maintain audit committee independence meeting SEC Rule 10A-3. It discusses Rule 10A-3 generally for audit committee members without tying that obligation explicitly to controlled companies.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States upon ceasing controlled status, majority of independent committee members required within 90 days
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions that controlled companies are exempt from some independence requirements, but it does not state that upon ceasing to be a controlled company a majority of independent committee members is required within 90 days. There is no 90-day transition/compliance timeframe discussed for committees after losing controlled status.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States all independent committee members required within one year and majority independent board within twelve months of ceasing controlled status
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions controlled companies being exempt and notes IPO phase-in periods, but it does not state the specific timing requirement that all independent committee members be in place within one year and that a majority-independent board be achieved within twelve months of ceasing controlled status.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States foreign private issuers may follow home country practices but must have audit committee meeting NASDAQ and SEC requirements
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says foreign private issuers may follow home country governance standards but must maintain an audit committee meeting SEC independence rules. However, it does not state that the audit committee must meet both NASDAQ and SEC requirements, so the NASDAQ requirement element is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States audit committee members are prohibited from accepting consulting, advisory, or other compensatory fees beyond board/committee fees
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that audit committee members “must not directly or indirectly accept consulting, advisory, or other compensatory fees from the company,” other than fixed retirement benefits, which matches the prohibition beyond board/committee fees.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States stock ownership alone does not preclude independence
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states, in the independence determination section, that “Ownership of company stock alone does not disqualify a director,” which conveys that stock ownership alone does not preclude independence.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Presents NYSE independence thresholds or rules as NASDAQ requirements without distinguishing between the two exchanges
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response repeatedly frames the thresholds (e.g., $120,000 compensation limit; $200,000 or 5% revenue business-relationship test) as “NASDAQ rules” and does not distinguish them as NYSE vs NASDAQ standards. It also cites NYSE/Nasdaq comparison sources but never clarifies which exchange each threshold belongs to.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: For at least 3 exemption types, states both what requirements are exempt AND what requirements still apply
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses exemptions for controlled companies, IPO phase-ins, and foreign private issuers, but it does not clearly state for at least three exemption types both (a) which specific independence requirements are exempt and (b) which specific requirements still apply. For example, controlled companies and IPOs are described only generally, without enumerating remaining obligations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes BDCs must comply with Rule 5600 Series as they are not exempt like registered management investment companies under 1940 Act
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not mention BDCs, Rule 5600 Series compliance, or the exemption for registered management investment companies under the 1940 Act. It only discusses general Nasdaq independence requirements and exemptions like controlled companies and foreign private issuers, so the specific BDC/1940 Act point is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly distinguishes compensation test exclusions (board fees, pension, non-discretionary) from business relationship exclusions (securities investments, charitable matching)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions compensation-test exclusions (e.g., director fees, retirement benefits) but does not explicitly distinguish those from separate business-relationship exclusions like securities investments or charitable matching. It also does not clearly identify securities investments and charitable matching as business relationship exclusions in contrast to the compensation exclusions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States all 3 controlled company phase-in timelines (immediate requirement, 90-day requirement, one-year requirement)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions that IPOs may have temporary relief or phase-in periods, but it does not state the three controlled company phase-in timelines (immediate, 90-day, and one-year requirements). It also does not enumerate any specific controlled company compliance deadlines. Therefore the required three timelines are missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly states audit committee has additional/enhanced independence tests beyond standard board-level criteria
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly says that for Audit Committee members NASDAQ imposes “more stringent” and “enhanced independence requirements” beyond general director independence, citing SEC Rule 10A-3 and listing additional fee/affiliate prohibitions. This directly states additional/enhanced tests for audit committee independence beyond standard board-level criteria.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies at least 6 distinct exemption categories with substantive explanation for each
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists only three exemption/special-case categories (controlled companies, certain IPOs, and foreign private issuers). It does not identify at least 6 distinct exemption categories, nor does it provide substantive explanations for six separate exemptions. The required minimum number of exemption categories is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains three-year lookback runs from relationship end and notes brief interim executive service exception
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response mentions a three-year lookback for employment/compensation/auditor ties, but it does not explain that the lookback runs from the end of the relationship. It also does not note the NASDAQ exception for brief interim executive officer service.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes binding obligations pre-dating November 2003 may exempt companies from independent nominating committee requirements
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses nominating committee oversight and exemptions for controlled companies, IPOs, and foreign private issuers, but it does not mention that binding obligations entered into before November 2003 may exempt companies from independent nominating committee requirements. The specific pre-November 2003 binding-obligation exemption is absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes between current auditor partner disqualification and former partner/employee who worked on company audit
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response treats the auditor restriction as a single rule (“cannot currently be, or have been in the past three years, a partner or employee of the company's outside auditor”) and does not distinguish between a current auditor partner disqualification versus a former partner/employee specifically tied to working on the company’s audit.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Treats controlled companies, foreign private issuers, and investment companies as having identical exemption scopes without distinguishing their different retained requirements
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response distinguishes exemption scopes by noting controlled companies are exempt from some board composition requirements, while foreign private issuers may follow home-country practices but still must maintain an SEC-compliant independent audit committee. It does not lump controlled companies, FPIs, and investment companies together as having identical retained requirements.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses formal legal terminology (executive officer, affiliated person, compensatory fees, disqualification criteria)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses formal legal terminology including “executive officer,” “compensatory fees,” “disqualification” language, and “affiliate”/“affiliate of the company” in the audit committee context.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes content with clear sections for definition, disqualifications, eligibility, and company requirements
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into distinct sections labeled “Definition,” “Eligibility Criteria,” “Disqualification Criteria,” and “Types of Companies Required to Have Independent Directors,” which correspond to the required definition, eligibility, disqualifications, and company requirements structure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective regulatory tone appropriate for compliance documentation without advisory recommendations beyond rule requirements
presentation-quality.explanation: The response generally uses a compliance-style, descriptive tone and focuses on NASDAQ rule criteria, but it includes advisory language such as “Well-governed Nasdaq companies regularly conduct detailed questionnaires…” which goes beyond stating rule requirements.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual expressions such as "basically", "get a pass", "who's in/out", contractions, or colloquial phrasing
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses contractions such as “cannot,” “must not,” “does not,” and “it’s” style phrasing throughout, which counts as casual/conversational language under the criterion.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same disqualification criterion or exemption multiple times using different wording without adding new regulatory detail
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats the same disqualification concepts in both the “Eligibility Criteria” and “Disqualification Criteria” sections (e.g., employment within three years, $120k compensation cap, auditor ties, cross-comp committee conflicts) with largely rephrased wording and little added regulatory detail.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2) for independent director definition and disqualification criteria
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses NASDAQ independence standards generally but never cites or references “NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(a)(2)” as the source for the independent director definition and disqualification criteria. It also does not explicitly tie the listed bright-line tests to Rule 5605(a)(2) by name/number.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(b)(1) for majority independent board requirement
citation-quality.explanation: The response states that a majority of the board must be independent but does not cite or mention NASDAQ Listing Rule 5605(b)(1) specifically. It references “Rule 5605” generally and provides other sources, but the exact rule citation required is absent.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites SEC Rule 10A-3 for audit committee independence requirements
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references “SEC Rule 10A-3” when describing the enhanced independence requirements for audit committee members and ties it to NASDAQ audit committee independence compliance.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NASDAQ Listing Rule 5615 or IM-5605 for exemptions or interpretive guidance
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses exemptions for controlled companies, IPO phase-ins, and foreign private issuers, but it does not cite NASDAQ Listing Rule 5615 or IM-5605 anywhere. It references Rule 5605 generally and SEC Rule 10A-3, but the specific required exemption/interpretive guidance citations are missing.

citation-quality.requirement: Relies exclusively on legal blogs, law firm marketing materials, or Wikipedia without citing primary NASDAQ rules or SEC regulations
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple secondary sources such as law firm materials and legal blogs, but it also cites Nasdaq Listing Center materials and references SEC Rule 10A-3, so it is not relying exclusively on blogs/marketing/Wikipedia without primary Nasdaq/SEC rules.
